nativeRange.habitat: Fancy Goldfish have been selectively bred for centuries from the wild-type Carassius auratus, and all commercial stock originates from breeding operations in China and Japan. The wild ancestor inhabits slow-moving rivers, ponds, and lakes, typically in eutrophic (nutrient-rich, low-oxygen) freshwater environments.

decorPreferences: smooth rounded substrate (sand or rounded gravel)
hardy plants or silk plants (avoid sharp decor that can tear fins)

diet.notes: Best fed sinking goldfish pellets to reduce air-gulping (floating pellets are associated with swim bladder issues in egg-body morphs). Supplement with blanched vegetables (peas, spinach, zucchini) and frozen/live foods such as brine shrimp and daphnia. Feed small amounts twice daily; avoid overfeeding.

avoidWith: fast-swimming single-tail goldfish (Comet, Common) — outcompete for food
fish with impaired vision (Bubble Eye, Telescope/Black Moor) should not share with aggressive feeders

summary: Fancy Goldfish is a collective name for selectively bred twin-tail, egg-body varieties including Oranda, Ranchu, Ryukin, Fantail, and Black Moor. These coldwater fish are intermediate to keep, with their compressed body shape making them prone to swim bladder issues and sensitive to water-quality lapses.
careNotes:
This entry covers all twin-tail, egg-body fancy goldfish varieties collectively. Individual variety entries: [oranda], [ranchu], [ryukin], [fantail-goldfish], [black-moor]. Cross-reference also [common-goldfish], [comet-goldfish], [shubunkin].

VARIETIES: This entry covers the Oranda (distinguished by a fleshy head growth called a wen and a twin tail), Ranchu (wen-covered head, no dorsal fin, and twin tail), Ryukin (a prominent high back hump and twin tail), Fantail (egg-shaped body with a quadruple caudal fin), Black Moor and Telescope Eye (both characterized by protruding eyes, the Black Moor in all-black coloration), Lionhead (an extreme wen that covers the entire head), Bubble Eye (fluid-filled sacs beneath upward-pointing eyes — extremely delicate and prone to injury), Celestial (upward-pointing eyes and no dorsal fin), Pearlscale (a nearly spherical body with distinctive domed scales), and Pom Pom (enlarged nasal tissue forming pom-pom-like lobes). The Veiltail is an additional variety notable for its exceptionally long, flowing fins.

SWIM BLADDER DISEASE: The compressed egg-body shape of these varieties displaces the swim bladder, making them particularly prone to buoyancy disorders. Common triggers include air-gulping from floating foods, constipation, bacterial infections, and rapid temperature changes. Sinking pellets are strongly preferred over floating foods. Feeding a blanched, shelled pea after a 24-hour fast is a widely used first-line home treatment for mild buoyancy issues.

IMPAIRED-VISION VARIETIES: Bubble Eye and Telescope/Black Moor have severely reduced vision and tend to struggle competing for food against faster or more agile fish. They are generally best housed with other slow-moving, similarly impaired varieties, or in a dedicated tank. Sharp or rough decor should be avoided, as it can damage their eye sacs or protruding eyes.

COLDWATER REQUIREMENT: Fancy goldfish are coolwater fish; temperatures consistently above 23–24°C are generally considered stressful for most varieties. A heater is typically not needed in climate-controlled indoor environments. They are not suited to life with tropical species, which require warmer water.

TANK SIZE: Fancy goldfish are heavy waste producers. 20 US gallons (approx. 76L) per fish is the widely cited minimum; 150L or more for a pair is generally advised, with additional space benefiting long-term health. Bowls and small containers are not appropriate for these fish.

FILTRATION: Strong biological filtration is important given their high ammonia output. Hang-on-back or canister filters fitted with low-flow outlets are generally recommended to avoid buffeting these slow-swimming fish.

LONGEVITY: Well-maintained fancy goldfish have been reported to live 10–20 years. Many individuals die prematurely when kept in inadequate conditions such as bowls, overly warm tanks, or with overfeeding.
breedingNotes: Fancy Goldfish are egg scatterers that tend to spawn in spring, or when water temperature begins to rise. Aquarium spawning can be triggered by gradually raising the temperature toward the upper end of their range. Eggs are adhesive and attach to plants or other surfaces; the adults will consume them if not separated promptly after spawning. Fry require very fine foods such as infusoria or commercial fry foods in the earliest days, graduating to finely crushed flake as they grow. Sexing is most straightforward during spawning season, when males develop small white breeding tubercles on their gill plates.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary goldfish care guide; covered tank sizing (20 gal/fish), temperature (50–70°F / 10–21°C), diet, and general husbandry. Dedicated fancy-goldfish URL returned 404; general care guide URL used.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (4–8 in / 10–20 cm), temperature (50–75°F / 10–24°C), pH (6.5–7.5), tank size (20 gal/fish), lifespan (5–10 yrs, up to 20 for Veiltail), temperament, compatible tankmates, varieties, and swim bladder disease info. Minor disagreement on temperature max (21°C vs 24°C) — used 23°C as average, consistent with user-supplied brief. Lifespan upper end aligned with user brief (10–20 yrs for well-kept specimens).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy (Cyprinidae / Cypriniformes), native range (East Asia, China, Japan), wild habitat description, wild adult size max 48 cm (not applicable to egg-body fancy varieties — used hobby sources for captive size), wild depth range 0–20 m. FishBase wild temperature (0–41°C) not used for aquarium parameters.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterers that will consume their own eggs if not removed from the spawning tank promptly. Spawning can be encouraged by gradually warming the water. Fry should be raised separately from the adults.
